resume:
Dana Whitfield — Commercial Counsel

Licensed: NY (2014), CA (2019)

Experience
Staff Counsel, Meridian Logistics (2019-present): commercial contracts, vendor and SaaS agreements, data-processing addenda.
Associate, Hale & VergER LLP (2014-2019): general commercial litigation and contract disputes.

Education
J.D., Fordham Law (2014). B.A. Political Science, Rutgers (2011).

Skills: contract drafting, redlining, DPA negotiation, limitation-of-liability, indemnity.
jd: Staff Commercial Counsel — B2B SaaS (Series D). You will own the contract lifecycle for enterprise deals above $250k ACV, partnering directly with Sales and Security to close customer paper on aggressive timelines. The role leads negotiation of MSAs, DPAs, and security exhibits, sets fallback positions on liability and indemnity, and builds the playbook that lets the deal desk self-serve on mid-market terms. You will report to the GC and be the sole legal partner to a 40-rep enterprise sales team. We need someone who can hold a hard line on uncapped indemnity while still unblocking revenue at quarter-end.

history.q: Tell me about the highest-stakes contract negotiation you personally led — what was at risk, what you did, and how it landed.

candidate_last_answer: Sure. Our largest prospect, a Fortune 100 retailer, demanded uncapped indemnity for any data incident, which was a deal-breaker for our risk posture and threatened a roughly $1.4M ACV deal at quarter-end. I owned the negotiation directly with their deputy GC. I proposed a super-cap structure: a 3x-fees cap for general liability but a separate, higher carve-out cap specifically for data-breach indemnity tied to our cyber-insurance limit, and I walked their team through our SOC 2 controls to justify it. I also coordinated with our CISO to get sign-off on the carve-out number before I put it on paper.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: before I put it on paper

expected-yield: the actual result — whether the counterparty accepted the super-cap, the final agreed cap numbers, and what the candidate would have conceded if the deputy GC had rejected the carve-out
